name: Columbia River (Oregon)
description:
The Columbia River forms the entire northern border of Oregon and stands as one of the greatest salmon rivers on Earth. Stretching 309 miles of shoreline across ten Oregon counties from the Pacific Ocean at Astoria to the high desert near Boardman, this massive waterway reaches depths of 200 feet and drains an area larger than France. The Columbia's legendary salmon runs have drawn anglers from around the world for generations, and its diverse fishery includes everything from trophy Sturgeon to hard-fighting Walleye.

Chinook Salmon fishing on the Columbia River is a Pacific Northwest tradition, with spring, summer, and fall runs providing year-round opportunities for anglers trolling herring and spinners in the main channel. Steelhead fishing heats up from late fall through winter, while Sturgeon attract catch-and-release enthusiasts targeting these prehistoric giants in the deeper pools below Bonneville Dam. Walleye and Smallmouth Bass have established thriving populations in the warmer waters east of the Cascades, and the annual Shad run each May brings fast-paced action near the dam faces.

Boat launches are abundant along the Oregon side of the Columbia, with major facilities in Portland, Hood River, The Dalles, and Astoria providing convenient access to different sections of the river. Whether you are chasing fall Chinook in the Buoy 10 fishery near the river's mouth, battling Steelhead in the Columbia Gorge, or casting for Smallmouth Bass in the John Day Pool, the Columbia River delivers an unmatched diversity of fishing experiences across the entire breadth of Oregon.

name: Willamette River
description:
The Willamette River flows 187 miles through the heart of Oregon's most populated region, winding through eight counties from Eugene northward through Salem and Corvallis before joining the Columbia River at Portland. This vital waterway reaches depths of 40 feet and serves as the lifeline of the fertile Willamette Valley, supporting both a thriving urban fishing scene and quiet rural stretches where anglers can fish in relative solitude. The river's remarkable restoration over the past several decades has transformed it into one of the best urban fisheries on the West Coast.

Spring Chinook Salmon are the premier target on the Willamette, with anglers trolling and back-bouncing from Oregon City upstream to the forks near Eugene each spring. Steelhead provide exciting winter fishing in the upper reaches and tributaries, while Sturgeon lurk in the deeper holes below Willamette Falls. Smallmouth and Largemouth Bass have exploded in population throughout the river, offering outstanding warm-water fishing from late spring through fall, and Walleye continue to expand their range from the lower river upstream.

Boat ramps are well distributed along the entire Willamette corridor, with excellent public facilities in Portland, Oregon City, Salem, Corvallis, and Eugene making it easy to access any section of the river. The Willamette's gentle current and relatively mild conditions make it ideal for drift boats, jet boats, and even kayak fishing. Whether you are anchoring up for spring Chinook below the falls at Oregon City or floating a summer stretch for Smallmouth Bass, the Willamette River offers accessible year-round fishing through the scenic valleys and cities of western Oregon.

name: Tillamook Bay
description:
Tillamook Bay is the second-largest estuary on the Oregon coast, offering 40 miles of shoreline in Tillamook County where five rivers converge to create one of the most productive saltwater fishing environments in the Pacific Northwest. With depths reaching 20 feet in the main channels, this expansive bay provides a protected environment for anglers to pursue salmon, crab, and bottomfish without venturing into the open ocean. The charming fishing village of Garibaldi serves as the primary gateway to the bay, with a historic waterfront lined with charter services and fish-cleaning stations.

Chinook and Coho Salmon fishing dominates the Tillamook Bay calendar, with fall runs drawing enormous crowds of anglers trolling spinners and herring in the bay's channels from August through November. Dungeness Crab fishing is equally popular, with recreational crabbers dropping pots throughout the bay's shallow flats and pulling limits of fresh crab from the nutrient-rich waters. Lingcod and Halibut are available just outside the bay's entrance at the Tillamook Bar, while Sturgeon fishing in the deeper channels provides year-round action for patient anglers.

Boat launches in Garibaldi, Bay City, and Tillamook provide excellent access to all areas of the bay, from the protected upper reaches where river currents mix with tidal flows to the deeper channels near the bar. Bank fishing opportunities are also plentiful along the bay's edges and on the jetties at the mouth. Whether you are crabbing on a calm summer morning, battling a bright Chinook in the fall tide, or bottom fishing for Lingcod near the jaws of the bay, Tillamook Bay delivers an authentic Oregon coast fishing experience.

name: Coos Bay
description:
Coos Bay is the largest deep-water coastal bay in Oregon and home to the largest coastal city on the state's shoreline, making it a major hub for both commercial and recreational fishing on the southern Oregon coast. With 35 miles of shoreline in Coos County and depths reaching 30 feet in the main shipping channel, this expansive estuary provides year-round fishing opportunities in protected waters just minutes from full-service marinas, tackle shops, and waterfront restaurants. The fishing village of Charleston, located near the bay's entrance, is the epicenter of the sport fishing fleet.

Chinook and Coho Salmon pour through Coos Bay each fall on their way to spawning tributaries, creating outstanding trolling and anchor fishing opportunities in the bay's channels. Striped Bass have established a strong population in the bay and its feeder rivers, providing exciting catch-and-release fishing from spring through fall. Dungeness Crab is a major draw, with the bay's productive mudflats and channels yielding generous harvests for recreational crabbers. Just beyond the bay's entrance, Lingcod and Rockfish inhabit the reefs and rocky structure along the coast.

Charleston Marina serves as the primary launch point for bay and offshore fishing, with excellent boat ramp facilities, fuel docks, and fish-cleaning stations. Additional launches in Coos Bay and North Bend provide access to the upper bay and river channels. Whether you are trolling for fall Chinook in the main channel, soaking crab pots on a rising tide, or running offshore from Charleston for bottomfish, Coos Bay offers the full spectrum of Oregon coast fishing from a convenient and well-equipped home port.

name: Yaquina Bay
description:
Yaquina Bay is the crown jewel of the central Oregon coast fishing scene, situated in the vibrant port city of Newport in Lincoln County. With 20 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 25 feet, this well-protected bay serves as home port to one of the largest sport fishing charter fleets in Oregon, offering anglers easy access to both excellent bay fishing and world-class offshore waters. Newport is also home to the Oregon Coast Aquarium and the Hatfield Marine Science Center, making it a premier destination where fishing and marine education go hand in hand.

Dungeness Crab fishing is a signature Yaquina Bay experience, with recreational crabbers pulling pots from docks, boats, and the bay's public crabbing pier throughout the season. Chinook Salmon run through the bay from late summer into fall, and the offshore fishery for Chinook, Lingcod, Rockfish, and Halibut is among the most productive on the entire Oregon coast. Surfperch provide excellent shore fishing along the nearby beaches and jetties, making Yaquina Bay a destination where every type of angler can find success.

The Port of Newport provides outstanding boat launch facilities, including the South Beach Marina and the Bayfront commercial port area, with ample parking and year-round access. Charter boats depart daily from the Bayfront for offshore trips targeting Halibut, Lingcod, and salmon. Whether you are pulling crab from the dock on a foggy morning, riding a charter boat through the Yaquina Bay bar to chase Halibut, or casting for Surfperch from the jetty, Newport and Yaquina Bay deliver an unforgettable Oregon coast fishing adventure.

name: Depoe Bay
description:
Depoe Bay holds the Guinness World Record as the smallest navigable harbor on Earth, yet this tiny Lincoln County port punches far above its weight as one of Oregon's most exciting and productive fishing destinations. The harbor entrance is a narrow channel carved through solid basalt, just 50 feet wide, through which charter and private boats navigate to access some of the richest nearshore fishing waters on the Pacific coast. Depths of 30 feet and beyond are found just minutes from the harbor mouth, where rocky reefs and kelp forests teem with marine life.

Lingcod and Rockfish are the bread and butter of the Depoe Bay fishery, with the nearshore reefs producing outstanding catches of these hard-fighting bottomfish throughout much of the year. Halibut fishing draws anglers from across the state during spring and summer openers, and Chinook Salmon trolling is productive from July through September as fish move along the coast. Dungeness Crab rounding out the harvest, with crabbers dropping pots in the waters just outside the harbor entrance.

Despite its diminutive size, Depoe Bay supports a fleet of charter boats that specialize in short-run offshore trips, making it possible to be fishing productive water within minutes of leaving the dock. The harbor's protected location and close proximity to fishing grounds also make it one of the safest departure points on the Oregon coast, with fewer bar crossings required than larger ports. Whether you are watching gray whales from the seawall between trips or heading out for a limit of Lingcod on a half-day charter, Depoe Bay offers a uniquely intimate Oregon coast fishing experience.

name: Rogue River
description:
The Rogue River is one of the original Wild and Scenic Rivers in America and stands as one of the most legendary steelhead and salmon streams on the Pacific coast. Flowing 215 miles through three southern Oregon counties from the Cascade Mountains near Crater Lake to the Pacific Ocean at Gold Beach, the Rogue carves through dramatic canyons and pristine wilderness that have captivated anglers, rafters, and adventurers for over a century. Its depths reach 30 feet in the deeper pools, and the river's consistent flows and diverse habitat support one of the healthiest anadromous fish runs in the state.

Steelhead fishing on the Rogue River is the stuff of legend, with both summer and winter runs attracting fly fishers and gear anglers to its crystal-clear waters. Chinook Salmon provide outstanding fishing from spring through fall, with the famous half-pounder Steelhead run in late summer offering fast-paced action that is unique to the Rogue system. Coho Salmon join the parade in autumn, while Smallmouth Bass and Rainbow Trout fill the warmer middle sections with year-round warm-water and cold-water fishing opportunities. Green Sturgeon inhabit the lower river near Gold Beach, adding an exotic element to the fishery.

Boat ramps and river access points are well distributed along the Rogue from Medford and Grants Pass downstream through the wild section to Gold Beach on the coast. Drift boats and jet boats are the preferred craft for navigating the river's mix of pools, riffles, and rapids, though bank access is excellent at numerous parks and public access sites. Whether you are swinging flies for summer Steelhead in the Holy Water section, trolling for Chinook in the tidewater near Gold Beach, or floating the wild canyon on a multi-day fishing trip, the Rogue River offers a fishing experience that is uniquely Oregon.

name: Umpqua River
description:
The Umpqua River system is one of Oregon's most treasured fishing destinations, with the North Umpqua fork earning worldwide recognition as a premier fly fishing stream for summer Steelhead. Flowing 111 miles through Douglas County from the Cascade Range to the Pacific Ocean at Reedsport, the Umpqua's two major forks and mainstem provide an extraordinary diversity of fishing opportunities across dramatically different landscapes. The river reaches depths of 20 feet in its deeper pools and runs, creating ideal holding water for anadromous fish throughout their seasonal migrations.

The North Umpqua is hallowed ground for fly anglers, with a 31-mile fly-fishing-only section that produces some of the finest summer Steelhead fishing in the world. The mainstem Umpqua below the forks offers excellent Chinook Salmon fishing from spring through fall, along with a growing Smallmouth Bass fishery that provides outstanding warm-water action during summer months. Shad runs bring fast and furious fishing each May and June, Striped Bass patrol the tidewater section near Reedsport, and Rainbow Trout fill the upper tributaries with opportunities for trout anglers seeking solitude.

Boat launches are available throughout the Umpqua system, with facilities in Roseburg, Elkton, and Reedsport providing access to different character stretches of the river. The North Umpqua's legendary fly water is best accessed through numerous pulloffs along Highway 138, while the mainstem is ideal for drift boats and jet boats. Whether you are presenting a dry fly to a summer Steelhead on the North Umpqua, anchoring up for spring Chinook near Elkton, or casting for Smallmouth Bass on a warm summer evening, the Umpqua River delivers a world-class fishing experience in the heart of southern Oregon.

name: Deschutes River
description:
The Deschutes River is Oregon's premier fly fishing destination and one of the finest trout streams in the American West. Designated as a National Wild and Scenic River, this 252-mile waterway flows through four central Oregon counties from its headwaters near Bend through dramatic basalt canyons to its confluence with the Columbia River. With depths reaching 15 feet in the deeper runs and consistent cold-water flows regulated by upstream dams and springs, the Deschutes supports an exceptional population of wild Redsides Rainbow Trout that are renowned for their size, strength, and willingness to take a dry fly.

Rainbow Trout fishing is the Deschutes's calling card, with wild Redsides averaging 12 to 16 inches and trophy fish exceeding 20 inches available throughout the lower river canyon. The section near Maupin is the most popular and accessible, with prolific hatches of salmonflies, golden stoneflies, and caddis drawing surface-feeding trout from May through October. Steelhead fishing from late August through November is equally legendary, with summer-run fish averaging 6 to 10 pounds providing heart-stopping takes on swung flies and drifted baits. Brown Trout, Smallmouth Bass, and Mountain Whitefish round out the diverse fishery.

Boat launches at Warm Springs, Trout Creek, Maupin, and numerous BLM access points provide excellent entry to the river's most productive stretches. Drift boats and rafts are the traditional way to fish the Deschutes canyon, though wade fishing from the well-maintained Deschutes River Trail offers outstanding bank access. Whether you are skating a dry fly over rising Redsides during a summer evening hatch, swinging a wet fly through a steelhead run at dawn, or floating the canyon on a multi-day camping trip, the Deschutes River is an Oregon fishing experience that belongs on every angler's bucket list.

name: Detroit Lake
description:
Detroit Lake is a stunning 3,580-acre Cascade mountain reservoir nestled in the Willamette National Forest along the North Santiam River corridor in Marion and Linn counties. With 32 miles of forested shoreline and an impressive maximum depth of 400 feet, this high-elevation lake is surrounded by towering Douglas fir and western red cedar and offers some of the best still-water fishing in western Oregon. The small mountain communities of Detroit, Gates, and Idanha provide rustic lodging, campgrounds, and services for visiting anglers.

Rainbow Trout and Kokanee Salmon are the primary targets at Detroit Lake, with stocked Rainbow Trout providing consistent action for trollers and bank anglers alike. Kokanee fishing is particularly popular from late spring through summer, with anglers trolling small dodger-and-fly rigs in the deeper water to fill their limits of these landlocked sockeye salmon. Landlocked Chinook Salmon grow to impressive sizes in the lake's deep, cold waters, and warm-water species including Largemouth Bass and Bluegill inhabit the shallower coves and arm areas during summer months.

Detroit Lake State Recreation Area and several U.S. Forest Service campgrounds provide excellent boat ramp facilities with paved launches and ample parking. The lake is conveniently located along Oregon Highway 22, making it an easy day trip from Salem or the Willamette Valley. Whether you are trolling for Kokanee on a summer morning with the Cascade peaks reflected in the still water, casting for Rainbow Trout from the shore at the state park, or exploring the lake's numerous coves and arms for bass, Detroit Lake delivers a quintessential Oregon mountain fishing experience.

name: Timothy Lake
description:
Timothy Lake is a 1,400-acre alpine reservoir set at 3,200 feet elevation in the Mt. Hood National Forest, offering one of the most scenic fishing experiences in Clackamas County. With 14 miles of shoreline backed by old-growth forest and views of Mt. Hood's snow-capped peak, this mountain lake reaches depths of 68 feet and provides cold, clear water that supports a thriving trout fishery throughout the summer season. The lake is accessible from Government Camp and Welches via Forest Service roads that wind through pristine Cascade wilderness.

Rainbow Trout are the most abundant gamefish in Timothy Lake, with generous stockings from ODFW supplementing natural reproduction to create excellent catch rates for trollers, fly anglers, and bait fishermen. Kokanee Salmon provide additional excitement for trollers working the deeper water with small wedding-ring spinners and corn. Brook Trout and native Cutthroat Trout inhabit the inlet streams and shallower portions of the lake, offering fly fishers the chance to catch beautifully colored wild fish in a high-mountain setting.

Several Forest Service campgrounds ring the lake, including popular sites at Gone Creek, Hood View, and Meditation Point, all of which provide boat launch access. The lake is well suited to small boats, canoes, and kayaks, with a 10 mph speed limit maintaining a peaceful atmosphere. Whether you are trolling for Rainbow Trout past the timbered islands, fly fishing the inlet streams for wild Brook Trout, or simply enjoying a quiet paddle with a fishing rod on a clear mountain morning, Timothy Lake is a Mt. Hood area gem that rewards every visit.

name: Odell Lake
description:
Odell Lake is one of Oregon's deepest natural lakes, plunging to 282 feet in the heart of the Cascade Range in Klamath County. This 3,582-acre glacial lake sits at 4,788 feet elevation along Highway 58 near Willamette Pass, with 13 miles of pristine shoreline framed by dense conifer forest and mountain peaks. The lake's extraordinary depth and cold, clear waters create ideal habitat for Lake Trout and other cold-water species, making it a destination fishery for anglers seeking trophy-class fish in a spectacular alpine setting.

Lake Trout, locally known as Mackinaw, are the premier target at Odell Lake, with fish regularly exceeding 10 pounds and trophy specimens reaching 30 pounds or more in the deepest portions of the lake. Kokanee Salmon are abundant and provide fast action for trollers during summer months, while Rainbow Trout offer consistent surface and near-surface fishing throughout the season. Bull Trout, a protected native char, inhabit the lake's deeper waters and are catch-and-release only. Mountain Whitefish round out the fishery and provide willing targets for fly anglers.

Odell Lake Lodge and Resort and several Forest Service campgrounds provide boat launch access and lakeside accommodations. The lake's location along Highway 58 makes it accessible from both Eugene and Bend, though winter access is limited by snow. Whether you are jigging for Mackinaw over deep structure, trolling for Kokanee through the lake's crystal-clear waters, or casting for Rainbow Trout along the timbered shoreline, Odell Lake offers a deep-water fishing adventure in one of Oregon's most beautiful Cascade settings.

name: Wickiup Reservoir
description:
Wickiup Reservoir is a 10,337-acre impoundment on the upper Deschutes River in Deschutes County, widely regarded as central Oregon's premier trophy Brown Trout fishery. With 42 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 70 feet when full, this high-desert reservoir sits amid the Ponderosa pine forests south of Bend and Sunriver, offering anglers the chance to catch truly massive Brown Trout in a scenic Cascade setting. The reservoir's extensive shallow flats and deep channels create diverse habitat that supports a remarkable variety of fish species.

Brown Trout are the star attraction at Wickiup, with the reservoir producing fish over 10 pounds each season and occasional specimens exceeding 20 pounds. These trophy browns feed heavily on Kokanee Salmon and Chub, growing to remarkable sizes in the reservoir's nutrient-rich waters. Kokanee themselves provide excellent fishing for trollers during summer, while Rainbow Trout and Mountain Whitefish offer consistent action throughout the season. Largemouth Bass and Crappie inhabit the warmer, shallower coves and provide outstanding warm-water fishing opportunities during the summer months.

Boat launches are available at several points around the reservoir, including facilities at Wickiup Butte, Gull Point, and the dam area, with Forest Service campgrounds providing lakeside camping and easy water access. The reservoir is a roughly 45-minute drive from Bend via Century Drive. Whether you are trolling plugs for trophy Brown Trout at first light, filling a limit of Kokanee during a summer afternoon, or casting for bass in the back coves, Wickiup Reservoir delivers big-fish potential and diverse angling opportunities in the high country of central Oregon.

name: Prineville Reservoir
description:
Prineville Reservoir is a 3,000-acre warm-water fishing destination tucked into the high desert canyon of the Crooked River in Crook County, offering some of the best bass and panfish fishing in central Oregon. With 43 miles of rocky shoreline and depths reaching 130 feet, this Bureau of Reclamation reservoir is surrounded by juniper-studded rimrock and sagebrush hills that create a striking contrast to the blue-green waters below. The city of Prineville, located 17 miles north, serves as the gateway to this popular fishery.

Largemouth and Smallmouth Bass are the primary draw at Prineville Reservoir, with both species thriving in the rocky structure and warm summer water temperatures. Bass fishing is particularly productive from May through September, with anglers working crankbaits, jigs, and soft plastics along the numerous rocky points and submerged ledges. Rainbow Trout provide excellent fishing during the cooler months when the reservoir's water temperatures favor cold-water species. Crappie, Catfish, and Bluegill round out the warm-water fishery, with Crappie stacking up in the brush and standing timber during spring.

Prineville Reservoir State Park and Jasper Point provide excellent boat ramp facilities with paved launches, courtesy docks, and ample parking. The state park also offers camping and day-use facilities right on the water. Whether you are working a crankbait along the rocky shoreline for Largemouth Bass, trolling for Rainbow Trout in the main body of the reservoir, or soaking bait for Catfish from the bank on a warm summer evening, Prineville Reservoir delivers reliable warm-water fishing action in the scenic high desert of central Oregon.

name: Lake Billy Chinook
description:
Lake Billy Chinook is a spectacular 3,997-acre three-arm canyon reservoir at the confluence of the Deschutes, Metolius, and Crooked Rivers in Jefferson and Deschutes counties. With 72 miles of shoreline carved into dramatic 500-foot basalt canyon walls and depths plunging to 415 feet, this is one of Oregon's most visually stunning fishing destinations. Cove Palisades State Park occupies a prime location at the junction of the three arms, providing outstanding recreation facilities and lake access near the towns of Madras and Culver.

Kokanee Salmon are the bread-and-butter fishery at Lake Billy Chinook, with trollers working the deep, cold waters of the Metolius and Deschutes arms for these hard-fighting landlocked salmon throughout the summer. Bull Trout, a protected native char, inhabit the lake's deepest waters and can reach impressive sizes feeding on the abundant Kokanee population, though they are strictly catch-and-release. Rainbow Trout and Brown Trout provide additional cold-water fishing opportunities, while Smallmouth and Largemouth Bass offer excellent warm-water action in the shallower Crooked River arm during summer months.

Cove Palisades State Park provides the primary boat launch facilities, with a full-service marina, boat ramp, and campgrounds in the Deschutes arm of the lake. Additional access is available at the Crooked River arm. The lake's canyon setting provides shelter from wind and creates a microclimate that allows fishing well into the fall season. Whether you are trolling the emerald-green waters of the Metolius arm for Kokanee, casting for Smallmouth Bass in the Crooked River arm, or simply marveling at the towering canyon walls from your boat, Lake Billy Chinook is a central Oregon treasure that delivers both world-class fishing and jaw-dropping scenery.

name: Diamond Lake
description:
Diamond Lake is a 3,015-acre high-elevation gem sitting at 5,183 feet in the Cascade Range of Douglas County, just a short drive from the north entrance of Crater Lake National Park. With 8 miles of shoreline encircling its nearly perfectly round basin and depths reaching 52 feet, this mountain lake is framed by the dramatic peaks of Mt. Bailey and Mt. Thielsen and is one of the most popular family fishing destinations in southern Oregon. The Diamond Lake Resort community provides lodging, boat rentals, and supplies for visiting anglers.

Rainbow Trout are the dominant gamefish at Diamond Lake, with ODFW maintaining a heavy stocking program that produces outstanding catch rates for anglers of all skill levels. The lake's nutrient-rich waters grow trout quickly, with fish averaging 12 to 16 inches and some reaching 20 inches or more by their second season. Tiger Trout, a sterile hybrid between Brown Trout and Brook Trout, have been introduced to help control invasive Tui Chub populations and provide an exciting bonus species with their distinctive vermiculated markings and aggressive feeding behavior.

Diamond Lake Resort and several Forest Service campgrounds provide boat launch facilities around the lake, with rentals available for those who do not bring their own watercraft. A paved bike path circles the entire lake, offering outstanding bank fishing access from virtually any point on the shoreline. Whether you are trolling a wedding ring spinner for Rainbow Trout on a calm mountain morning, casting from the shore with the kids, or targeting Tiger Trout with streamers near the weed edges, Diamond Lake delivers easy-access fishing in a world-class Cascade mountain setting just minutes from Crater Lake.

name: Upper Klamath Lake
description:
Upper Klamath Lake is the largest natural lake in Oregon, spanning an impressive 61,543 acres in Klamath County with 87 miles of shoreline along the eastern flank of the Cascade Range. With depths reaching 50 feet, this vast shallow lake sits at 4,143 feet elevation and is fed by the pristine waters of the Wood River, Williamson River, and numerous springs that create one of the most unique freshwater ecosystems in the Pacific Northwest. The city of Klamath Falls anchors the southern end of the lake, while the small communities of Rocky Point and Chiloquin provide access to the western and northern shores.

Rainbow Trout fishing at Upper Klamath Lake is legendary among fly anglers, with the Williamson River and its tributaries producing some of the largest wild Rainbow Trout in Oregon. Fish exceeding 10 pounds are caught each season, with the lake's abundant food supply of Tui Chub and aquatic insects fueling exceptional growth rates. Yellow Perch provide consistent panfish action throughout the lake, particularly in the shallow bays and along weed edges. Largemouth Bass inhabit the warmer, weed-choked coves and offer excellent fishing during summer months.

Boat launches are available at several locations around the lake, including facilities at Howard Bay, Rocky Point, and the Williamson River delta. The lake's vast open waters can produce dangerous wind-driven waves, so anglers should monitor weather conditions carefully and stay close to shore during rough conditions. Whether you are sight-fishing to giant Rainbow Trout in the crystal-clear Williamson River, trolling the open lake for Yellow Perch, or exploring the marshy backwaters for Largemouth Bass, Upper Klamath Lake offers a big-water fishing experience on a truly Oregon-sized scale.

name: Snake River (Oregon)
description:
The Snake River forms Oregon's eastern border and carves Hells Canyon, the deepest river gorge in North America at over 7,900 feet from rim to river, through the rugged wilderness of Wallowa, Baker, and Malheur counties. With 180 miles of shoreline and depths reaching 150 feet in the canyon pools, this powerful river offers some of the most remote and dramatic fishing in the Pacific Northwest. From the high desert near Ontario to the wild canyon accessible only by jet boat or foot trail below Joseph, the Snake River provides an extraordinary range of fishing environments and species.

Smallmouth Bass are the most popular gamefish in the Snake River's Oregon waters, with the warm, rocky habitat of Hells Canyon producing exceptional numbers of hard-fighting bronzebacks that average 12 to 16 inches. Chinook Salmon and Steelhead make epic migrations through the canyon, with fishing permitted during open seasons managed by ODFW. White Sturgeon inhabit the deepest pools, with catch-and-release fishing available for these ancient giants. Channel Catfish and Crappie provide additional variety, particularly in the slower waters near Ontario and in the reservoir sections.

Access to the Snake River's Oregon side ranges from easily reached boat launches near Ontario to remote wilderness launch points that require significant travel on gravel roads. Jet boat tours and outfitter-guided trips from Hells Canyon Dam provide the most popular access to the canyon's best fishing water. Whether you are jet-boating through the wild rapids of Hells Canyon to cast for Smallmouth Bass, drifting for Steelhead in the canyon's emerald pools, or fishing for Catfish from the bank near Ontario, the Snake River delivers wilderness fishing in one of the most awe-inspiring landscapes in the American West.

name: Siuslaw River
description:
The Siuslaw River winds 40 miles through Lane County from the Coast Range foothills near Mapleton to the Pacific Ocean at the charming coastal city of Florence, creating a productive estuary and river system that offers diverse saltwater and freshwater fishing opportunities. With depths reaching 15 feet in the main channels, this mid-sized coastal river flows through second-growth forest and past the towering sand dunes of the Oregon Dunes National Recreation Area, providing a picturesque setting for anglers pursuing salmon, crab, and a variety of other species.

Chinook Salmon are the premier target on the Siuslaw, with fall runs drawing anglers to the tidewater and lower river from September through November. Coho Salmon follow close behind, and Steelhead provide winter fishing action from December through March in the upper river and tributaries. Striped Bass have established a fishable population in the lower river and estuary, offering exciting catch-and-release opportunities during summer months. Dungeness Crab and Surfperch round out the saltwater side of the fishery, with crabbing particularly productive in the lower estuary near the jetties at Florence.

Boat launches in Florence and Mapleton provide convenient access to both the tidewater estuary and the upper river sections. The Port of Siuslaw in Florence offers a full-service marina with fuel, supplies, and a well-maintained boat ramp. Whether you are trolling for fall Chinook in the tidewater, pulling crab pots near the river's mouth, or drifting the upper river for winter Steelhead through moss-draped forests, the Siuslaw River offers a complete Oregon coast fishing experience anchored by the welcoming community of Florence.

name: John Day River
description:
The John Day River is the longest undammed river in Oregon and one of the longest free-flowing rivers in the contiguous United States, stretching 281 miles through six central and eastern Oregon counties across a vast landscape of painted hills, basalt canyons, and high desert rangeland. With depths rarely exceeding 10 feet, this shallow, wadeable river flows through some of the most remote and sparsely populated country in the state, offering anglers a wilderness fishing experience that feels truly wild and untouched. The small towns of John Day, Fossil, and Spray serve as remote outposts for anglers venturing into this expansive watershed.

Smallmouth Bass are the John Day River's most popular gamefish, with the warm summer water temperatures and abundant rocky habitat producing phenomenal numbers of aggressive bronzebacks throughout the lower and middle river sections. Steelhead migrate up the river each fall and winter, providing outstanding fishing in the upper sections for anglers willing to make the trek to remote access points. Wild Chinook Salmon are present but fishing opportunities are limited by conservation closures. Rainbow Trout and Mountain Whitefish inhabit the cooler upper reaches, providing additional fly fishing opportunities in pristine mountain stream settings.

Boat launches are available at scattered points along the river, with popular put-in sites at Service Creek, Spray, Clarno, and Cottonwood Bridge facilitating multi-day float trips through the canyon sections. The John Day is an outstanding float-fishing river, with gentle Class I and II rapids connecting long pools and runs that are ideal for casting to Smallmouth Bass from a drift boat or raft. Whether you are floating a remote canyon stretch casting poppers to eager Smallmouth Bass, wading the upper forks for wild Rainbow Trout, or planning a multi-day wilderness float through the Painted Hills, the John Day River offers a raw and unforgettable Oregon fishing adventure far from the crowds.
